Machine Learning for Characterization and Processing
Unit 1: What makes materials data special?

AI 4 Materials / KI-Materialtechnologie

Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz

FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg

01. Welcome to AI 4 Materials

  • Goal of the Course: Moving from “textbook AI” to “lab-ready AI”.
  • Focus on Characterization (images, spectra) and Processing (time-series, logs).
  • Why materials science needs its own ML flavor.
  • Prerequisites: Mathematical Foundations of AI (MFML).

02. The “Hype Cycle” vs. Reality

  • AI is often equated with LLMs (ChatGPT) or self-driving cars.
  • In Materials Science, the challenge is often “Small Data” and “High Dimensionality”.
  • We need models that are not just accurate, but physically plausible.
  • Expectation: “Just throw data at it.”
  • Reality: “Garbage in, garbage out” is magnified in the lab.

03. Why Now? The Convergence

  • High-Throughput Experiments: Robotic microscopy, automated synthesis.
  • Advanced Simulation: Phase field, FEM, DFT as data generators.
  • Compute Power: GPU acceleration for deep learning.
  • The Missing Link: Scientists who can bridge the gap between physics and algorithms.

04. Learning Outcomes for Unit 1

  • Understand the difference between White-Box and Black-Box models.
  • Identify the unique scales and modalities of materials data.
  • Relate ML tasks to the Processing–Structure–Property–Performance (PSPP) paradigm.
  • Learn the basics of the CRISP-DM workflow adapted for labs.

05. Defining a “Model” in Engineering

  • First-Principle Models (Bottom-Up):
    • Derived from physical axioms (e.g., Navier-Stokes, Schrödinger).
    • High interpretability, high computational cost.
  • Data-Driven Models (Top-Down):
    • Extracted from observations (e.g., ML regressors).
    • High speed, but often “Black-Box”.

06. The Spectrum of Models

  • White-Box: Fully explainable, physically derived.
  • Grey-Box: Hybrid models (e.g., PINNs).
  • Black-Box: Empirical mappings (e.g., standard CNNs).
  • Goal: Moving toward “Grey” or “White” using domain knowledge.

07. Historical Perspective: Overfitting in Antiquity

  • Aristotle’s Geocentric Model: 56 nested spheres to explain planetary motion.
  • It “fit” the data perfectly but was fundamentally wrong.
  • Occam’s Razor: The simplest explanation is often the best.
  • Lessons for ML: Avoid over-parameterizing your model for small datasets.

08. What students bring from MFML

  • You know Linear Algebra: PCA, SVD, Matrix operations.
  • You know Probability: Bayes’ Theorem, distributions.
  • You know Optimization: Gradient Descent, Loss functions.
  • Now: We apply these to noisy, biased, and multi-scale experimental signals.

09. 90-Minute Roadmap

  1. Introduction & Philosophy (Where we are)
  2. Models in Engineering (Data-driven vs. Physics-based)
  3. The PSPP Paradigm (The structure of materials data)
  4. Data Foundations (Types, scales, and uncertainty)
  5. The CRISP-DM Workflow (How to execute a project)

10. Initial Discussion: A Failure Story

  • Case Study: An ML model predicts material failure with 99% accuracy.
  • The Twist: It was actually learning the serial number of the testing machine, not the physics.
  • Data Leakage: The biggest silent killer in lab-based ML.
  • Prompt: How do we ensure our model learns the “right” features?

11. The PSPP Paradigm: Our Data Map

  • Processing: Temperature, pressure, cooling rate.
  • Structure: Grain size, phase fractions, dislocation density.
  • Property: Yield strength, conductivity, ductility.
  • Performance: Service life, corrosion resistance.
  • ML Task: Mapping the arrows between these circles.

12. Task Mapping: Processing → Structure

  • Inputs: 1D process logs (time-series).
  • Outputs: 2D Micrographs or 3D volumes.
  • Challenges: The time delay between processing and observation.
  • Example: How does quenching rate affect martensite lath width?

13. Task Mapping: Structure → Property

  • Inputs: Micrographs (pixels), Spectra (vectors).
  • Outputs: Scalar properties (Hardness, \(K_{IC}\)).
  • Challenges: High-dimensional inputs, limited labels.
  • Example: Predicting fracture toughness from SEM crack patterns.

14. What Makes Materials Data Special? (I)

  • Multi-Scale: From atoms (\(10^{-10}\) m) to airplane wings (\(10^1\) m).
  • Multi-Modal: Pixel grids (SEM), Orientation maps (EBSD), Chemistry (EDS).
  • Spatial Correlation: Pixels in a micrograph are NOT independent.
  • Anisotropy: Properties depend on direction (crystallography).

15. What Makes Materials Data Special? (II)

  • Small Data: Labeled samples are expensive (days of lab work).
  • Biased Data: We only publish “successes,” leading to “Success Bias”.
  • Experimental Noise: Shot noise, thermal drift, sample preparation artifacts.
  • Metadata: Data is meaningless without units and sample history.

16. Foundations of Data: The 4 Scales

  1. Nominal: Names (e.g., “Steel”, “Aluminum”).
  2. Ordinal: Ordered (e.g., “Fine”, “Medium”, “Coarse”).
  3. Interval: Difference matters (e.g., \(0^\circ\)C vs \(10^\circ\)C).
  4. Ratio: Absolute zero exists (e.g., \(0\) K, \(0\) MPa).
  • Warning: Do not calculate means on ordinal data!

17. Nominal vs. Cardinal Data

  • Nominal (Categorical): Grouping without numerical meaning.
  • Cardinal (Metric): Values you can add, subtract, and multiply.
  • Binary: The simplest case (Yes/No, Defect/No Defect).
  • Normalization: Different scales require different preprocessing.

18. Measurement Uncertainty

  • Aleatoric: Inherent randomness (e.g., quantum noise in a detector).
  • Epistemic: Ignorance (we don’t have enough data).
  • Metadata: Always record the instrument resolution and error bars!
  • ML models should ideally predict a distribution, not just a number.

19. Instrument Dependence & Domain Shift

  • A model trained on SEM “A” may fail on SEM “B”.
  • Even the same instrument drifts over time.
  • Domain Adaptation: Ensuring the model generalizes across labs.
  • Mini-case: Different contrast settings lead to different segmentations.

20. Checkpoint Question

  • Question: You have a dataset of alloy compositions and their grain sizes. What is the data scale of “Alloy Name” and “Grain Size (µm)”?
  • Answer: Alloy Name = Nominal; Grain Size = Ratio.
  • Follow-up: Can you use a standard MSE loss on Nominal data?

21. Introduction to CRISP-DM

  • CRoss-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining.
  • A systematic cycle to ensure you don’t just “play with code”.
  • Adapted for the materials lab: Scientific Understanding first.

22. Phase 1: Business (Scientific) Understanding

  • What is the material physics question?
  • What are the costs of a False Positive vs. False Negative?
  • Is there a physics-based “baseline” to beat?

23. Phase 2: Data Understanding

  • Visualizing raw data (EDA: Exploratory Data Analysis).
  • Checking for outliers and measurement artifacts.
  • Assessing label quality: “How sure is the expert who labeled this?”

24. Phase 3: Data Preparation

  • Cleaning: Handling NaNs, outliers, and duplicates.
  • Normalization: Scaling features to \([0, 1]\) or mean \(0\), variance \(1\).
  • Feature Engineering: Adding physical descriptors (e.g., aspect ratio).

25. Phase 4: Modeling

  • Selecting the algorithm (Random Forest vs. CNN vs. GP).
  • Setting the loss function (MSE, Cross-Entropy).
  • Hyperparameter tuning.

26. Phase 5: Evaluation

  • Does the model generalize? (Cross-Validation).
  • Does the model make physical sense? (Sensitivity analysis).
  • Benchmarking against the status quo.

27. Phase 6: Deployment (Lab Integration)

  • Integrating the model into the instrument control software.
  • Real-time decision making.
  • Monitoring for model drift.

28. Correlation != Causality

  • Ice Cream and Crime: Both increase in summer, but ice cream doesn’t cause crime.
  • Materials Example: Cooling rate and hardness are correlated, but microstructure is the causal mechanism.
  • Always check if your ML features are physically linked to the target.

29. Baseline-before-Complexity

  • Always start with a Linear Regression or a Simple Tree.
  • If a deep NN only improves accuracy by 1% over a linear model, use the linear model.
  • Simple models are easier to explain and harder to break.

30. Recap: Unit 1 in a Nutshell

  • Materials data is multi-scale, multi-modal, and sparse.
  • Trust the physics as much as the data.
  • Use CRISP-DM to stay disciplined.
  • Next week: How signals are actually formed (Physics of data formation).

31. References & Further Reading

  • Neuer (2024): Ch. 1.1 + 1.3 (CRISP-DM perspective)
  • Sandfeld (2024): Ch. 2.1 (Domain knowledge integration)
  • McClarren (2021): Ch. 1 (Landscape of ML for Engineers)
  • Murphy (2012): Ch. 1 (Introduction)

© Philipp Pelz - ML for Characterization and Processing
